abstract:
Risk is an inherent and inescapable feature of all human endeavors. Within the context of business, it represents a variable that can never be entirely eliminated. A defining characteristic of risk management is the recognition that risk can only be mitigated—or its negative consequences minimized—under specific operational conditions.

The classification of risk remains an expansive and complex challenge. Academic literature offers various taxonomies, typically tailored to the specific functional goals of the classification system. At its core, risk categorization involves assigning specific threats to established groups based on predefined parameters. In this paper, the authors synthesize the theoretical foundations of risk categorization, focusing particularly on the risks associated with commercial and industrial activities. The primary objective is to provide a comprehensive synthesis of theoretical frameworks that contributes to the advancement of risk management as a rigorous scientific discipline.
